coredata.dc:title: Preparation and luminescent modulation of yellow carbon dots for electroluminescent device
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Yanan
Li, Xu
Zheng, Zhiyong
Chen, Mingjun
Zhang, You
Liu, Zhenyang
Wang, Fenghe
Guan, Li
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots have become the strong competitive materials for the light-emitting layer material of electroluminescent devices due to their lower toxicity and without heavy metal ions. In this experiment, bright yellow-orange fluorescent carbon dots have been synthesized by one-step solvent thermal method and the fluorescence is increased by adjusting the pH, which has excitation independent characteristics. Simultaneously, the solvent effect relating to the polarity of the solvent and the surface functional groups are investigated by the variety of emission peaks in different solvents. Based on its excellent optical properties, the electroluminescent devices were prepared using the mixture of as-prepared carbon dots and PVK as an emissive layer. The photoelectronic properties of the carbon dots-based device were measured and the luminance can reach 220 cd/m2, and the color coordinate was located at (0.30, 0.65), which provided a solid foundation for the application of carbon dots in photoelectric devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Electroluminescence
Solvation effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The morphology and structure of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) CDs preparation process, (b) TEM image of CDs (the inset is the particle size distribution diagram); (c) HRTEM image.
(a) FTIR spectrum of EtOH plus KOH group and EtOH group; XPS spectrum of CDs; (b) C1s spectrum, (c) N1s spectrum, (d) O1s spectrum of the CDs.

(a) The absorption, excitation and emission spectrum of CDs; (b) the PL spectrum under different excitations (305–445 nm) and the inset is fluorescent photo of CDs; (c) the normalized PL emission; (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs in different solvents. (cyclohexane, ethyl acetate, acetone, EtOH, CH3OH).

(a) The schematic structure and (d) The energy level diagram of CDs-ELD. (c) The normalized EL spectra at different voltages, and the inset is the photography of CDs-ELD, (d) The J-V-L characteristics curve of CDs-ELD.

head.ce:title: Preparation and luminescent modulation of yellow carbon dots for electroluminescent device

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory of Optic-electronic Information and Materials
College of Physics Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots have become the strong competitive materials for the light-emitting layer material of electroluminescent devices due to their lower toxicity and without heavy metal ions. In this experiment, bright yellow-orange fluorescent carbon dots have been synthesized by one-step solvent thermal method and the fluorescence is increased by adjusting the pH, which has excitation independent characteristics. Simultaneously, the solvent effect relating to the polarity of the solvent and the surface functional groups are investigated by the variety of emission peaks in different solvents. Based on its excellent optical properties, the electroluminescent devices were prepared using the mixture of as-prepared carbon dots and PVK as an emissive layer. The photoelectronic properties of the carbon dots-based device were measured and the luminance can reach 220 cd/m2, and the color coordinate was located at (0.30, 0.65), which provided a solid foundation for the application of carbon dots in photoelectric devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a rising star of the carbon family, have become a research hotspot because of their many outstanding advantages, including excellent optical properties, great photostability, good biocompatibility, ease of synthesis, without heavy metals and low toxicity [1–4]. To date, CDs have been applied to various fields, such as optoelectronic devices [5,6], bioimaging [7], security [8], sensors [9] and photocatalysis [10]. The preparation technology and properties of CDs have developed rapidly in the past decades, but most of the luminescence colors of CDs previously reported are limited to short-wavelength emission (blue or green), which limits its broader application [11,12].

In order to achieve tunable emission, the method of changing particle size and chemical surface states of CDs is usually adopted. For example, Xiong's group reported a method for preparing panchromatic emission CDs using p-phenylenediamine and urea as precursors, which can change the luminescence color from blue to red by adjusting the surface states [13]. Qu and co-workers used different solvents to control the degree of dehydration and carbonization of the precursor and changed the particle size to control the bandgap emission of CDs, leading to different colors of their emission ranging from blue to red [14].

In addition to using the luminescent properties of CDs to prepare light-conversion light-emitting diodes (LEDs), it had become the most promising application in the display field for employing CDs as a light-emitting layer in electroluminescent device (ELD) [15,16]. Compared with other materials, the CDs have unique color-tunable photoluminescence properties due to the size effect, surface effect, and edge effect [17,18]. There have been many successful examples of ELD based on CDs (CDs-ELD). Kang et al. fabricated pure blue CDs-ELD using O, N co-doped blue CDs and PVK (poly(N-vinylcarbazole)) as the light-emitting layer, which the CIE coordinates are (0.14, 0.10) and the external quantum efficiency (EQE) was as high as 2.11% [19]. Liu's research group synthesized a blue-emitting S-CDs with graphite structure using phthalic acid and ethylenediamine via a one-step hydrothermal approach, and used it as a light-emitting layer to prepare a CDs-ELD with a maximum brightness of 4.97 cd/m2 [20]. However, the long-wavelength CDs that can be used in CDs-ELDs are rarely reported.

In this work, yellow-orange CDs were successfully synthesized using neutral red as the precursor and EtOH as the solvent by the one-step solvothermal method. The fluorescence enhancement was achieved in an alkaline environment by adjusting the pH. The as-synthesized CDs had excitation-independent characteristics and solvation effect. Based on outstanding light-emitting performance, the host-guest doping mechanism was adopted for preparing CDs-ELD with a brightness of 220 cd/m2 and a color coordinate of (0.30, 0.65). This provided a great demonstration of CDs in the field of ELD and played an important role in the application of monochromatic displays.

The raw material neutral red (C15H17ClN4, >90%) was purchased from Macklin. Ethyl alcohol (EtOH) (99.7%), Cyclohexane (99.7%), Ethyl acetate (99.5%), Methanol (99.5%), acetone (99.0%) and isopropanol (99.7%) were purchased from Tianjin Kemiou and Damao. HCl, NH4OH and KOH were purchased uniformly by the laboratory. All chemicals are used directly without further purification. The water used in the experiment is deionized water.

The synthesis process of CDs by one-step solvothermal method was as follows: 0.35 g of neutral red was dissolved in 12 mL of EtOH, at the same time, 3 mL KOH solution was added to adjust its pH value. After stirring for half an hour, the mixture was transferred into a 25 mL poly(para-phenol)-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The reactant was heated at 200 °C for 8 h and then cooled to room temperature using a drying box. Subsequently, the resultant solution was taken out and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the large particles. Thereafter, the obtained solution was purified via silica column chromatography using acetone as the eluent. Finally, a relatively pure yellow-orange CDs solution was obtained and preserved in acetone solution.

The light-emitting layer of ELD: 15 mg/mL CDs in toluene solution and 15 mg/mL PVK in toluene solution are used in a volume ratio of 1:3.5, wherein the molecular weight of the polymer PVK is 25,000–50000.

The ITO (indium tin oxide)-coated substrates were scrubbed using glass liquid and deionized water to remove the impurities, and sonicated with acetone and isopropanol for 30 min, separately. The ITO was oxidized with ozone for 30 min in order to decompose the residual solvent on the ITO surface and reduce the potential barrier. After that, the PEDOT:PSS (poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene): poly(styrene-sulfonate)) (AI 4083) was spin-coated on ITO at 5000 rpm for 40 s, and then annealed in air at 150 °C for 30 min. After cooling to room temperature, the ITO substrates were transferred to a nitrogen glove box. The TFB (poly (9,9-dioctylfluorene-coN-(4-(3-methylpropyl)) diphenylamine) (8 mg/mL chlorobenzene solution) (ADS 259BE) was spin-coated on the PEDOT:PSS layer at 3000 rpm for 40 s. Then annealed at 150 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, a light-emitting layer doped with PVK and CDs was spin-coated on the TFB at 2000 rpm for 40 s. Finally, the devices with different functional layers were placed in a thermal evaporator, and TPBi (1,3,5-tris (Nphenylbenzimidazol-2-yl) benzene) (70 nm), Liq (8-Quunilinolato lithium) (1 nm) and Al (aluminum) (100 nm) are deposited in a vacuum of less than 5 × 10−4 Pa. The active area of devices was 2 mm × 2 mm. The devices were then encapsulated with a cover glass fixed with low-permeation epoxy resin.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CDs was obtained by a JEM 2100plus transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) with a working voltage of 200 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) was detected by employing an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB250Xi, Thermo Fisher). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on an FTIR spectrophotometer (ALPHA, BRUKER) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The absorption spectra were recorded on Lambda 950 ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded with a fluorescence spectrofluorometric (F-7000, Hitachi). The Raman spectroscopy was tested by HR Evolution (Horiba Jobin Yvon) using a laser wavelength of 532 nm. The lifetimes were recorded on a multifunction steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrophotometer (1712c-FL, HORIBA). All the measurements were completed at room temperature.

The electroluminescence (EL) spectra were taken using an Ocean Optics USB 2000 spectrometer with the devices driven at a constant current using a Keithley 2400 source meter. The current density-voltage-luminance (J-V-L) characteristics of ELDs and EQE values were recorded on a Keithley 2400 source meter and Photo Research-735.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology and structure of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The typical synthesis route is shown in Fig. 1 (a). Neutral red is selected as the carbon source and EtOH as the solvent for exploring the effects of different pH levels on the product and the as-prepared CDs could be homogeneously dispersed in most organic solvents. Four groups of EtOH plus HCl, EtOH, EtOH plus NH4OH and EtOH plus KOH are selected for the experiment. It can be found from Fig. S1 that the PL intensity of the acid group is very low and there was a weak peak at 482 nm under the same concentration and excitation at 365 nm. In contrast, the PL intensity increased after the alkali is added especially in the EtOH plus KOH group, while, the peak positions at 590 nm of the latter three groups are hardly moved. Thus, the EtOH plus KOH group is selected for the next experiment and the EtOH group is used as the comparison group.

TEM image and the corresponding histogram of size distribution histogram are shown in Fig. 1(b). It can be found that the CDs are well distributed and the average size of the CDs is about 6.5 nm. The HRTEM image in Fig. 1(c) shows that the lattice space is about 0.21 nm corresponding to (002) lattice fringes. It can be proved that the obtained CDs are structured like crystalline graphite carbon with uniform morphology and good dispersion [21].

The XRD pattern of CDs in Fig. S2 exhibited a broad diffraction peak centered at about 23.8°, which further confirms the graphene structure and the (002) crystal plane of graphite carbon of CDs [22]. The interlayer spacing of (002) diffraction facet increases to be 0.38 nm, which is larger than that of graphite (0.33 nm). The increase in the interlayer spacing is due to the fact that the surface of CDs contains abundant functional groups, and oxygen-containing groups are introduced after the oxidation process, resulting in the increase of the interlayer spacing [20,23].

Furthermore, the FTIR is used to investigate the surface functional groups of CDs in Fig. 2 (a). The CDs possess absorption bands of O–H/N–H stretching vibration (3355-3500 cm−1), C–H stretching vibration (3000-2900 cm−1), C = Ο stretching vibration at 1722 cm−1 [24], N–H bending vibration at 1627 cm−1, and C–O stretching vibration at 1205 cm−1, indicating the existence of amino groups and carboxyl groups and other chemical bonds in the CDs and further shows that it owns rich surface states and contains a variety of functional groups. It is worth noting that the content of C–H and CO in CDs increased after KOH is added, which is attributed to the increasing of oxidation degree and carboxylation [24]. The discovery of XPS is carried out to further identify the components of CDs. As shown in Fig. S3, the full spectrum includes three typical peaks: C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (400 eV) and O 1s (531 eV), indicating that the CDs are composed of four elements: C, N, O and H. In the high-resolution spectrum C 1s in Fig. 2(b), four peaks can be fitted, corresponding to sp2 carbon (CC/C–C, 284.6 eV), sp3 carbon (C–N, 285.2 eV), carbonyl carbon (CO, 286.2 eV) and carboxyl carbon (COOH, 288.0 eV) [25]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2(c)) can be decomposed into three peaks at 398.5, 399.2, and 400.0 eV, corresponding to N–H, C–N–C, and N-(C)3, respectively [26,27]. In Fig. 2(d) the O1s spectrum can be fitted to three peaks at 531.6, 532.3 and 533.2 eV belonging to CO, C–OH and C–O, respectively [28]. Through the XPS spectrum, it can be known that the as-prepared CDs have a rich variety of functional groups, illuminating that CDs have good solubility, in great agreement with the FTIR results [13].

Raman scattering is further used to reveal the structural characteristics of CDs. As shown in Fig. S4, the Raman spectrum of the CDs shows two peaks at 1373 and 1536 cm−1 corresponding to D bands and G bands, respectively. The intensity ratio of characteristic Raman bands (IG/ID) can be used to study the crystallinity and relative abundance of carbon core and surface atoms, and its ratio is directly proportional to the crystallinity [27]. The value of IG/ID is about 1.14 in this experiment, revealing that the CDs have good crystallinity and graphitized structure, which is consistent with the HRTEM and XRD results [29].

The UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra are adopted to investigate the optical properties of the acquired CDs. In Fig. 3 (a), two absorption peaks at 277 nm and 458 nm are observed. The absorption peak at 277 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition in the carbon core, while the peak at 458 nm mainly belongs to the defect state n-π* transition, which is often related to the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups CO. It is usually the cause of long-wavelength emission and refers to the surface states and bandgap of CDs [30]. The PL spectrum shows an emission peak at 600 nm under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, indicating a yellow emission. It can be found from Fig. 3(b) the PL intensity sharply increases and then decreases when the excitation wavelength shifts from 305 nm to 445 nm. The emission intensity reaches the maximum when excited at 365 nm, which is consistent with the excitation spectrum. Additionally, there is hardly movement of the emission, which indicates that the as-prepared CDs have excitation-independent properties. The excitation independent character is caused by the homogeneous microstructures of the surface chemical states of the CDs, which made them uniformly exist in the lowest singlet state [31]. Furthermore, excitation independent often occurs in long-wavelength emission and short-wavelength emission CDs often have excitation-dependent phenomena [32]. The results demonstrate that the surface states should be primarily responsible for the PL properties. Analogously, the fluorescence lifetime decay of the CDs is investigated by Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) and shown in Fig. S5(a). The lifetime over the whole emission spectra can be fitted into two exponential components as shown in Eqs. (1) and (2) (supporting information). The fluorescence behaviors are on the order of nanoseconds, indicating that the CDs had only fluorescent properties, with the average lifetime being 3.51 ns [33]. The quantum yield (QY) measured by dispersing CDs in EtOH with an integrating sphere is 16.7%.

Moreover, it is worth noting that the spectra of the prepared CDs in different solvents will change accordingly. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the strongest emission peak gradually shifts from 527 nm to 600 nm with increasing solvent polarity (CH3OH > EtOH > acetone > ethyl acetate > cyclohexane), thus resulting in the fluorescence color change from green to orange. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs in different solvents are shown in Fig. 3(d), and the absorption peaks at long wavelengths are located at 423, 427, 430, 440 and 460 nm, respectively. The results prove that different solvents polarity will induce the changes of surface states and the surface states are involved in the emission process [34]. Analogous to the red shift of the PL spectrum, the absorption peaks of the CDs also exhibit a marked red shift with increasing solvent polarity. These observations clearly demonstrate CDs uniquely solvent dependent characteristics [35]. According to previous reports, the solvent dependence of CDs in protic solvents is mainly due to the hydrogen bond interaction between protic solvents and CDs, and the dipole-dipole interaction mainly affects the PL shift in different aprotic solvents [35,36]. Meanwhile, the lifetime in different solvents of CDs is explored and given in Fig. S5(b), in which the lifetime in different solvents is different, indicating that the solvents have a degree of impact on CDs.

Equally, the change in PL intensity of CDs is detected in solutions with the pH values increasing from 1 to 13 in Fig. S6(a). It can be found that different pH values have effect on the PL intensity of CDs and the optimal PL intensity occurs when pH = 7. Simultaneously, the PL intensities of CDs at different storage times are also measured. As shown in Fig. S6(b), the PL intensity can be well maintained during the 30 days storage period, indicating the CDs have excellent stability. The strong fluorescence and great photostability of CDs make them have good potential for being applied in electroluminescent devices.

The excellent PL emission characteristics of the as-prepared CDs make them be applied in ELDs. The schematic diagram of the ELD structure using Al as the cathode and ITO films as the anode is shown in Fig. 4 (a). Al/Liq film is the electron injection layer (EIL), and PEDOT:PSS film is the hole injection layer (HIL). TPBi as the electron transport layer (ETL) is used for electron transmission and TFB is used as hole transport layer (HTL). A CDs & PVK film was used as an emissive layer in sandwich-structured ELDs. Pure CDs as the light-emitting layer usually cannot produce high-purity CDs-ELD, which is because the CDs will undergo fluorescence quenching due to aggregation and PVK can improve the dispersion of CDs [37]. Consequently, the luminescence quenching of CDs-ELD can be effectively suppressed by doping CDs into PVK as an electron-transporting layer. The schematic diagram of the energy levels in Fig. 4(b) is adopted to explain the synergy between the functional layers. The energy level of as-prepared CDs is calculated by the Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) (Fig. S7(a and b)). The HOMO level of CDs can be obtained by UPS is −5.40 eV. Taking the photon energy (hv) as the horizontal axis and (αhv)2 (α is the absorption intensity) as the vertical axis to redraw the UV–vis absorption curve (Fig. S7(c)) [38], it can be deduced that E
g is 2.27 eV, then the LUMO level value is −3.13 eV. The energy level values for other materials are referred to from the literature [39]. It can be found that the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy level between ITO and PEDOT:PSS and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level between Al/Liq and TPBi are very close, so that the carriers can easily pass through the barrier to achieve charge balance [40]. The energy level of CDs is located within the energy level of the PVK, the transferred electrons and holes can undergo radiative recombination in CDs to produce electroluminescence.

The EtOH group is selected as the comparison group to prepare ELDs. According to Fig. S8(a), there is a strong blue emission peak at 440 nm and a very weak emission peak belonging to CDs at 530 nm, which may be due to its weak binding ability to excitons, most of the excitons radiate on TFB, and low luminous brightness and poor luminous performance of this group of CDs, thus causing the device to show blue emission. The shape of the EL spectrum did not change with voltage. As for its high current density in Fig. S8(b), it may be due to exciton quenching. In addition, it can be seen from Fig. S8(c and d) the calculated HOMO value is 4.66 eV, which is quite different from the energy level value of the adjacent layer, resulting in a large potential barrier that is not conducive to carrier transmission. While the CDs prepared with the addition of KOH and PVK are used as a functional layer, the performance of the ELD significantly improves. As expected, the CDs-ELD can emit green light at 13–17 V. A green emission peak at around 530 nm is observed in the EL spectra in Fig. 4(c) and a weak blue emission peak at 435 nm corresponds to the blue emission from TFB [41]. The position of the peaks hardly changes with the increase of the voltage. The turn-on voltage is 9.9 V, the larger turn-on voltage of the device may be due to that the surface ligands of CDs affect the transport performance of CDs, which resulting in the imbalance of hole and electron injection [37,42]. As the CDs concentration increases, the turn-on voltage of the device will gradually increase. The luminous performance is the best when the mixing ratio of CDs and PVK is adjusted to 1:3.5. It can be seen in Fig. S9, the PL spectrum of CDs has the same shape as its corresponding EL spectrum, just that the peak position slightly shifts. Furthermore, the maximum luminance of the ELD is 220 cd/m2 in Fig. 4(d), the highest EQE is up to 0.18% (Fig. S10(a)) and the CIE coordinates are (0.30,0.65) in the green color region in Fig. S10(b).

In conclusion, the yellow CDs were successfully synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method using neutral red as the carbon source and EtOH as the solvent. By adjusting the pH, it was found that alkali can increase the degree of carbonization and improve the optical properties of CDs. The prepared CDs exhibited yellow emission independent of excitation, with a QY of 16.7%. Besides, it has been observed to have a solvating effect. As the polarity of the solvent increases, the emission peak red shifts. Because of its bright and high-purity luminescence characteristics, it is applied to the ELD with a mixture of CDs and PVK as the light-emitting layer. The CDs-ELD after pH adjustment exhibited yellow-green light emission as we expected at 540 nm, with a maximum brightness of 220 cd/m2 and a CIE coordinate of (0.30, 0.65). The successful preparation of the CDs-ELD strongly confirmed the huge potential of CDs in ELDs applications.

Y. N. Ding and X. Li: synthesize, characterize the prepared CDs and prepare electro-induced devices. Z. Y. Zheng: assisted in device preparation and research. M. J. Chen and Y. Zhang: assisted in the test and provided useful discussions. F. H. Wang and L. Guan and Z. Y. Liu provided useful discussions and revised the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 61974009, 62075055, 62175057), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. 2020201030), Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality (No. Z210018), Foundation of Graduate Innovation Funding Project of Hebei University (HBU2021ss065), Interdisciplinary research project of Hebei University (No. DXK202012) and Advanced Talents Incubation Program of the Hebei University (No. 521000981236). We also appreciate the financial support from the high-performance supercomputer center of Hebei University.
